content:
Some comments on the compiler intrinsics/builtins and where to find docs/examples:

1. In most cases builtin translates into intrinsic. In most cases translation is 1:1. But not always. Many intrinsics directly or nearly directrly map to the ISA manual / shader programming guide insts
2. LLVM user guide https://llvm.org/docs/AMDGPUUsage.html
3. ISA https://www.amd.com/content/dam/amd/en/documents/instinct-tech-docs/instruction-set-architectures/amd-instinct-cdna4-instruction-set-architecture.pdf
4. Builtins: https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/main/clang/include/clang/Basic/BuiltinsAMDGPU.td
5. Intrinsics descriptions https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/main/llvm/include/llvm/IR/IntrinsicsAMDGPU.td
6. Code examples: https://github.com/carlushuang/gcnasm/tree/master
7. https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/llvm/test/CodeGen/AMDGPU
8. https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/clang/test/CodeGenOpenCL and https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/clang/test/SemaOpenCL
9. grep llvm repo
10. experiments

Subject: Seeking clarity on mxfp4-mm task boundaries

Hi everyone! I'm a newcomer using AI to help bridge the gap between my current logic and the ROCm/Triton implementation.

I first heard about this competition on r/LocalLLaMA and decided to give it a shot. I'm not sure if I’ll succeed, but I figured if I don’t try, I'll never know. I've done some preliminary searching and noticed discussions about "defense_kernel_hack" and warnings against cheating. To stay within the "fair play" zone, I'd like to clarify the intended boundaries for the mxfp4-mm task:

Core Objective: Is the goal purely to minimize the latency of the GEMM execution, or is it a "full pipeline" challenge including quantization and shuffling?

Legality of B_shuffle: The provided data tuple includes B_shuffle. Is it within the spirit of the task to use this pre-processed data directly? I noticed a significant jump (~18µs vs ~34µs) when I handle the weight transformation manually.

Scope of Customization: Are we allowed to replace the aiter defaults with our own custom Triton kernels or even inline ASM?

Level of Customization: Are we allowed to use things like CUDA/HIP graphs or auxiliary C++ functions to bypass Python overhead?

I want to make sure I'm focusing my optimization efforts on the intended hardware bottlenecks without accidentally "reward hacking." Thanks for any guidance!
---
I'm currently developing on an Arch Linux machine with an E3 1230v2, 24G DDR3 RAM, and a Vega 64 (gfx900). Since my local hardware doesn't natively support some of the newer FP4 kernels, I'm relying heavily on the Popcorn CLI / MI355X remote environment to validate my work.

From what i understood anything is permissible as long the code compute truly matrix multiplication and did not calculated in advance or cheated like the blog u saw. So hip, c++, as long u achieve to run it the popcorn cli good work
